citation.authors: Divband Soorati, M., Clark, J., Ghofrani, J., Tarapore, D., & Ramchurn, S. D.
citation.title: Designing a User-Centered Interaction Interface for Human–Swarm Teaming

extraction_metadata.notes: Contains two distinct human-subjects components (a 100-participant online visualization-preference study, and a 15-participant technology-probe interface evaluation) plus a separate simulation-only swarm model (Experiments I-III) with no live human in the loop; coded as genuinely mixed per the Study-Type codebook's Section 2 comparable-weight rule. Re-coded 2026-07-10 against Task-Environment v0.8: this article is the codebook's own cited source example for both the new Static/Fixed Task Object (3.4) and Search Task (4.2) codes, which replace the original two Unclear / Candidate New Code flags.

study_type.justification: Two comparable-weight human-subjects components are reported (a 100-participant online visualization-preference study, Section 2; a 15-participant remote technology-probe interface evaluation, Experiment IV), so User Study is Primary; the belief/confidence-map interface and human-swarm collective decision-making model are framed as a general, reusable 'testbed' and 'benchmark' for the field, comparably weighted, so System/Architecture Paper is Secondary.

summary.task: In a simulated disaster-monitoring mission zone, an operator worked with up to 15 simulated UAVs to map a disaster area, either through fully autonomous exploration with monitoring only (Experiments I-II, one of which features a disaster that relocates mid-run), through issuing area-level attraction/repulsion commands including marking permanent no-fly zones (Experiment III), or, in a separate live study (Experiment IV), by using the interface interactively to guide exploration toward a moving disaster under two control schemes. A separate, earlier online study (Section 2) asked 100 participants to compare individual-UAV-dot versus heat-map swarm-coverage visualizations after watching short videos, with no live task or control involved.
summary.control: Two control paradigms were compared in Experiment IV: an 'indirect parameter setting' confidence-map interface (Task I), where the operator manipulates their own local belief/confidence heat map by clicking cells to mark areas of interest or no-fly zones, which then diffuses through one-to-one neighbor communication to bias each UAV's potential-field-based motion, without the operator ever selecting or addressing an individual UAV; and direct individual-UAV target assignment (Task II), where the operator selects a specific UAV from a list and assigns it a target by clicking a map cell.
summary.participants: Two distinct samples: (1) 100 online participants (62 female, 37 male, 1 non-binary, minimum BSc-level qualification), ethically approved, for the visualization-preference study; (2) 15 computer scientists (BSc/MSc in Computer Science or Engineering) recruited from a small online community for the technology-probe interface evaluation, given remote access to the simulation machine and briefed/debriefed via 10–15-minute video calls — an informal probe study with no stated formal power analysis.
summary.results: Heat maps were significantly preferred over individual-UAV displays for larger swarms, for displaying motion/coverage, and for time-critical tasks (all p < 0.001 after Bonferroni correction), and produced significantly higher System Usability and System Acceptance scores (both p < 0.001); individual-UAV displays were preferred only for detecting errors in the swarm. In the interface evaluation, the confidence-map interface (Task I) produced belief-map accuracy comparable to manually assigning individual UAVs (Task II), and both human-guided conditions outperformed fully autonomous exploration once the disaster moved (via operator-timed interventions, i.e., 'neglect benevolence'), while Task II was reported as requiring substantially higher cognitive workload than area-only targeting.
summary.issues: Individual-UAV displays remain preferable specifically for error detection, suggesting heat maps alone may be an insufficient sole display. Individual-UAV target selection (Task II) imposed markedly higher cognitive workload than area-only targeting and does not scale to larger swarms. No parameter-sensitivity analysis was performed (swarm size, confidence decay factor, and local/global weighting were all fixed and chosen empirically). Mapping resolution necessarily decreases as the explored area grows, limiting scalability. The technology-probe study (Experiment IV) was informal, with only 15 self-selected participants and no formal statistical comparison reported between Task I and Task II.
summary.recommendations: Combine both display types — use heat maps as the core display but retain an individual-UAV view as a supplementary diagnostic tool for error detection. Extend the model to multiple simultaneous dynamic disasters and multiple human operators with collaborative/competitive strategies, and to detecting adversarial 'intruder' UAVs or operators. Investigate communication/positioning/sensing noise and malfunctioning/lost-UAV detection. Study speed-versus-accuracy tradeoffs under different messaging qualities. Run a systematic parameter-sensitivity analysis rather than relying on empirically fixed values. Co-design the interface with industry experts and conduct a larger, more comprehensive user study. Investigate operator-bias effects in human-in-the-loop experimentation. Support directing a subset of the swarm via a more selective communication protocol. Extend the approach to physical UAVs and real-world evaluation.
summary.autonomous_capabilities: Each UAV autonomously builds and shares local belief and confidence heat maps via one-to-one neighbor communication (no full network connectivity required), decides its own motion by descending the gradient of a Sobel-filtered confidence potential field (attracted to low-confidence/unexplored cells, repelled from boundaries and neighbors' recently-visited high-confidence cells for implicit collision avoidance and spatial division of labor), and absorbs a temporarily-injected operator command that diffuses through the swarm via the same reliability-weighted neighbor-averaging mechanism used for ordinary belief updates, with a locking mechanism that temporarily preserves the operator's influence against being averaged away. The swarm can run with full autonomy or under partial operator influence, self-organizing to redistribute individual target points without the operator specifying which UAV performs which sub-task.

task_object_spatiotemporal_dynamics.justification: The disaster zone explicitly relocates from one point to another between simulation steps 150-350 in Experiment II, and again in Experiment IV, requiring the swarm/operator to continuously re-track its position.
The multi-barrier test uses up to five separate, fixed-location permanent no-fly zones scattered in the arena, each a static, self-contained instance.

In Experiment I the disaster is a single, fixed, initially-unknown location that the swarm must discover and localize; it does not move or grow once found — matching Section 3.4 (Static/Fixed Task Object), added in Task-Environment codebook v0.8, for which this article is the codebook's own cited source example.
The Section 2 visualization-preference study is a passive video-watching comparison with no live task or task-relevant object being acted upon.

behavioral_relation_to_task_object.justification: The operator applies a repulsion force to keep the swarm away from marked no-fly/prohibited zones, with contact (flying over the zone) being the thing to prevent.
Locating and mapping the disaster area is a search relation (finding the object is the goal), not avoidance — matching Section 4.2 (Search Task), added in v0.8.

team_size.justification: One operator interacts with a swarm of 15 UAVs throughout the simulation experiments and the technology-probe study.
The Section 2 visualization-preference study is a passive individual video-comparison task with no live human-robot team.

team_composition.justification: Although the model supports heterogeneous agent capabilities in principle, all 15 UAVs in the actual experiments share identical parameters (φ = 0.5, ρ = 1) and behavior.

interaction_model.justification: By design, the operator communicates only with UAVs in their own local neighborhood, whether disseminating a confidence-map command or assigning a target to a specific selected UAV; this is explicitly framed as keeping cognitive complexity at O(1).

interaction_directness.justification: The operator's command is explicitly disseminated to the UAVs within their own local communication neighborhood.

UAVs beyond the operator's direct neighborhood are affected by the operator's influence only via relayed, averaged confidence values passed hop-by-hop between neighboring UAVs, not by any message addressed to them.

communication_proximity.justification: The operator interacts entirely through the confidence-map interface on a remote machine running the simulation (Experiment IV participants were given remote access to the simulation machine), with no physical co-location with real UAVs.

coordination_architecture.justification: No robot or server holds coordinating authority; each UAV independently computes its own motion from locally-shared, reliability-weighted confidence averages with its neighbors, with the operator entering the same peer-to-peer averaging process rather than issuing centrally-coordinated commands.

computational_characteristics.justification: UAV motion is generated by an explicitly named artificial-potential-field method (local and global confidence gradients computed via a Sobel filter, weighted and summed), a deterministic geometric computation with no learned model or population-based search.

human_involvement_level.justification: The swarm explores, maps, and (when commanded) redirects itself without requiring continuous human input under any condition tested — fully autonomous exploration functions correctly with the operator only monitoring, and the optional attraction/repulsion commands are consistent with 'flexible autonomy' in which the human can intervene but the system does not depend on it to operate.

level_of_human_control_abstraction.justification: In both Task I (area-level attraction/repulsion click) and Task II (per-UAV target-cell click), the operator gives a discrete goal for the swarm/robot to achieve, and the potential-field controller autonomously executes all steps to reach it, with no operator safety-monitoring role described.
level_of_human_control_abstraction.condition: Experiment III/IV, both Task I and Task II

operational_domain.justification: All agents in the model are UAVs operating in the air domain (over a 2D mission zone at constant altitude).

flags.note: The Section 2 visualization-preference study has no live task-object at all (participants only watched pre-recorded videos and answered questionnaires).
Same reason as above — Section 2's passive video-comparison study has no live human-robot team to describe.
